title: Tag-Along Virtual Windows Increase Perceived Resistance and Task Load in Augmented Reality
mini_abstract: We show that head-following (Tag-Along) AR windows systematically amplify pseudo-haptic resistance and increase perceived workload compared to a fixed (Head-Locked) baseline.
abstract: Head-following virtual windows can keep content within view in augmented reality (AR), but their motion may introduce a pseudo-haptic sense of resistance during head movement. This paper examines how Tag-Along window behavior influences perceived resistance and task load relative to a Head-Locked baseline, across multiple window configurations. Results indicate that Tag-Along behavior robustly increases perceived resistance and elevates NASA-TLX workload, suggesting a trade-off between visibility support and interaction comfort when designing AR window-following dynamics.
contributions: Establish pseudo-resistance as a measurable perceptual effect induced by head-following (Tag-Along) AR windows, relative to a Head-Locked baseline.
Quantify the impact of window-following dynamics on subjective workload using NASA-TLX, highlighting consistent workload increases under Tag-Along behavior.
Derive practical design implications for AR window-following policies, clarifying when Tag-Along behavior may hinder comfort and productivity-oriented use.
results: Tag-Along windows produce significantly higher perceived resistance than the Head-Locked baseline.
NASA-TLX workload is consistently higher under Tag-Along behavior across task conditions.
The adverse effects of Tag-Along behavior are amplified under certain window configurations, indicating an important design trade-off between persistence and comfort.

title: Sign Language Vocabulary Recognition Only with Tactile Sensing Glove
mini_abstract: JSL finger-spelling recognition using a 46-sensor tactile glove achieves 87% accuracy with k-NN and Dynamic Time Warping.
abstract: We present a tactile sensing glove with 46 force-sensitive Velostat sensors for Japanese Sign Language (JSL) vocabulary recognition. Using LSTM and k-NN with Dynamic Time Warping on 10 JSL finger-spelling signs collected from 5 participants, we achieve test accuracies of 76% and 87% respectively, demonstrating the potential of sensor-based SLR without reliance on vision-based approaches.
contributions: Developed a low-cost, portable tactile glove with 46 Velostat pressure sensors strategically placed at finger joints and fingertips
Designed a preprocessing pipeline with baseline correction, moving average filtering, and Min-Max normalization for cross-subject generalization
Compared LSTM and k-NN with DTW on a 10-class JSL finger-spelling dataset using group 5-fold cross-validation
results: k-NN with Dynamic Time Warping achieved 87% average accuracy (Precision 0.88, Recall 0.87, F1 0.85)
LSTM achieved 76% test accuracy; dropout and L2 regularization were applied to reduce overfitting on the limited dataset
